titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Emery and Elvera Carlson Papers
titlestmt.author: Martha Lund Smalley

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday October 25, 2018 at 22:03

did.unittitle: Emery and Elvera Carlson Papers
did.abstract: Correspondence, writings, and collected material document the work of Emery W. Carlson and Elvera Teed Carlson, Lutheran missionaries serving in China from 1940 to 1949. Emery Carlson was a medical doctor who worked at the Lutheran Hospital in Xuchang, Henan (Hsuchang, Honan) province from 1941 to 1944. He was active in famine relief work 1942-1944 and in 1945 became an intelligence officer working for the Office of Strategic Services behind Japanese lines in Henan. After the war Carlson returned to the mission hospital but the family was forced to leave China in 1949 due to the Communist takeover.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Emery and Elvera Carlson Papers (RG 246), Special Collections, Yale Divinity School Library.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Emery W. and Elvera Teed Carlson were Swedish-American missionaries in China serving under the Evangelical Lutheran Augustana Synod in North America (later known as the Augustana Evangelical Lutheran Church.) Emery Carlson was a medical doctor at the Lutheran Hospital in Hsuchang, Honan province [Xuchang, Henan] from 1941-1944. He was active in famine relief work 1942-1944 and in 1945 served as an intelligence officer for the Office of Strategic Services behind Japanese lines in Honan province. He was allowed to return to his medical work after the war ended but the family was forced to flee when the Communists took control of the province in 1947-1948 and returned to the United States in 1949.
For more detailed biographical information see In War and Famine : Missionaries in China's Honan Province in the 1940s by Erleen Carlson Christensen.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These papers, which constitute a major portion of the source material used by Erleen Christensen in writing In War and Famine : Missionaries in China's Honan Province in the 1940s , tell the story of Swedish-American Lutheran missionaries serving in Henan province during a turbulent period in China history. The Sino-Japanese war, Nationalist Communist conflict, banditry, and famine due to crop failures and inflation were dominant forces during the Carlsons' relatively brief but intensive period of work in China.

Series I, Correspondence, is divided into Family and General Correspondence. The Family correspondence contains detailed letters written by the Carlsons to their extended family in the U.S., and well as letters written by Emery to his wife during their wartime separation. Daily life for the family and Emery's medical work are described, as well as the frequent moves and separations occasioned by political events. In the General correspondence section, circular letters written by the Carlsons and letters to/from their mission provide additional documentation, including regarding the American School Kikungshan. For additional documentation regarding the American School Kikungshan, a Lutheran school missionary children, see YDSL Record Group No. 164.
Series II, Writings, includes journals by Elvera and Emery as well as published articles related to the Augustana Evangelical Lutheran Church's work in China.

Series III, Biographical Documentation, includes newspaper clippings about the Carlsons as well as documentation of Emery's work for the OSS during World War II. Series IV, Collected Material, concludes the collection with material related to fellow missionaries, programs, brochures and photos.

corpname.part: Augustana Evangelical Lutheran Church ( Missions. China..)

did.unittitle: Correspondence
Family correspondence
Extended family

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These letters were written by Emery and Elvera Carlson to extended family members in the U.S.

did.unittitle: Leaving U.S. and travel to China
Arrival in China; studying Chinese in Peiping (Beijing)
Evacuated to Honan (Henan) province to avoid Japanese troops
Travel to Hsuchang, Honan (Xuchang); continuation of language study
Incl. re. mission conference, daily life
Descriptions of daily life of missionaries
Incl. re. medical work of Emery W. Carlson (EWC)
Descriptions of EWC's clinical work, daily life
Incl. re. difficulty getting supplies, unsettled conditions; description of Chinese Postal Service
Description of hospital care; language exams; Elvera T. Carlson (ETC) teaching English at girls' school
EWC takes bicycle trip to Juchow and Loyang to check on medical work; threats from Japanese troops
Family moves to Juchow in January; EWC only doctor at hospital; visits other stations; daughter Faith Louise born March 12.
Rampant famine and inflation; EWC made chair of relief committee; girl babies being abandoned
Family moves to Hsuchang; EWC only doctor in mission; much travel by bicycle, treating patients; better harvest brings some relief to famine
Japanese invasion necessitates move west with dangerous journey; family settles in Chungking (Chongqing); son Jon Paul born July 10
Waiting in Chungking; EWC takes family to India to await transport to U.S.
EWC now civilian employee of U.S. Army, returns to Chengtu (Chengdu); ETC and children go to U.S.
EWC sets up dispensary in Sian (Xian), then Tantouchen, seeing 100 patients daily; when war ends, returns to Honan to re-equip hospital
EWC on furlough in U.S. with family, then family returns to China, stationed in Hsuchang after delays; threats from Communist troops
Hsuchang attacked by Communist troops; families moved to Chengchow (Zhengzhou), then Hankow (Wuhan), to await relocation; EWC travels to Kunming to explore establishing new work there
Carlsons and two other families move to Kunming
EWC doing clinical work in government camps for beggars and evangelistic work; son Mark Luther born August 26

Difficult economic conditions; older daughter Erleen at school in Hong Kong where American School Kikungshan had been evacuated; EWC working at clinic of Municipal Hospital in Kunming; Carlsons return to U.S. where EWC begins medical practice in Nebraska
Emery to Elvera

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These letters were written by Emery Carlson to his wife when they were separated from each other during wartime.

did.unittitle: EWC serving as civilian employee of U.S. Army during war; leaves family in India and returns to Chengtu
Descriptions of EWC's daily life in Chengtu
Incl. re. EWC socializing with other missionaries, some medical work; family has arrived back in U.S.
EWC seeing about 100 patients daily in Tantouchen
War ends; EWC considering plans for future; decides to return to Hsuchang via Chungking
EWC in Hsuchang; Japanese remaining on mission property, which is in bad condition; Japanese officer lends EWC truck for trip to Sian to get hospital equipment
Cleaning up hospital in Hsuchang; reorganizing work; many obstacles; longs to be with family
EWC on United Nations Relief and Rehabilitation Administration (UNRRA) committee; description of medical work
EWC begins trip home to U.S.; waiting for transport in Shanghai; on freighter diverted to Guam
General Correspondence
Circular letters from Carlsons
Augustana Board of Foreign Missions: letters to extended family regarding Carlsons
Augustana Board of Foreign Missions: regarding financial matters
Augustana Board of Foreign Missions: bulletins regarding Carlsons and other missionaries
American School Kikungshan: letters regarding school board matters and regarding daughter Erleen
Letters from Carlsons to/from others, including Alice Karel, Dr. Benson, Arthur Kennedy, Dr. Shih
Letters of others, including extended family members and other missionaries
Writings
Published articles, including "Through No Man's Land", "The Bible in China", "A Tour of the Augustana Synod Mission Field in Central Honan"
Journal of Elvera Carlson: "Impressions of My Work in China"
Journal of Emery Carlson: "The Work in Yunnan Province, China"
Biographical Documentation
Newspaper clippings regarding the Carlsons
Office of Strategic Services (OSS) documents, including contract of employment, certificate
Travel documents, innoculations,etc.
Collected Material
Prayer booklet, magazine articles, clippings, photos regarding other missionaries
Miscellaneous items, including program, booklets
